title: SEGMENT 15 — Endogenous retroviruses: the viruses that became us

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Endogenous retroviruses: the viruses that became us
Now I want to take you somewhere unexpected.
So far we've talked about retroviruses as things that infect cells, integrate into chromosomes, and produce new infectious particles.
But what if integration happens into a germline cell — a cell that will produce sperm or egg?
Then the provirus is passed on to the next generation.
It is no longer an infection.
It is a heritable allele.
This has happened, in our ancestors, many, many times.
The result is that the human genome contains an enormous amount of retroviral DNA.
The number is striking: about 8 percent of your genome is the rotted-out remains of ancient retroviral infections.
These are called endogenous retroviruses, or ERVs — in humans, HERVs, for human endogenous retroviruses.
For comparison, protein-coding genes make up about 1 to 2 percent of your genome.
There is, by mass, several times more retroviral DNA in you than there are genes.
Most HERVs are dead.

They have accumulated mutations, deletions, frameshifts; they cannot replicate; they have no LTR-driven transcription; many are reduced to solo LTRs, where the internal coding sequence has been removed by recombination between the two LTRs of a provirus, leaving a single LTR scar in the chromosome.
There are tens of thousands of these scars in our genome.
But some HERVs retain function — and a few of them have been co-opted for host purposes.
The most striking example is syncytin.
Syncytin-1 is a protein essential for the formation of the placenta.
The placenta has, on its maternal-facing surface, a layer called the syncytiotrophoblast — a multinucleated giant cell formed by the fusion of many trophoblast precursors.
That fusion is mediated by syncytin-1, a fusogenic membrane protein that brings cells together and merges them.
Syncytin-1 is encoded by the env gene of a HERV called HERV-W, which integrated into a primate ancestor about 25 to 40 million years ago.
The env gene of a retrovirus exists, of course, to fuse the viral membrane with a target cell membrane — that's how the virus enters a cell.
The cellular host repurposed that same fusion machinery to fuse trophoblast cells into a syncytium for the placenta.

Syncytin-1 in humans, syncytin-2 from another HERV insertion, and analogous syncytin proteins in other mammalian lineages — independent retroviral co-options that all produced the same placental cell-fusion architecture.
Without syncytin, no placenta.
Without placenta, no live birth as we know it.
So a retroviral protein, captured from an ancient infection, became absolutely essential for mammalian reproduction.
You are here, biologically, in part because of an endogenous retrovirus.
There are other examples.
Many HERV LTRs have been co-opted as enhancers or promoters for cellular genes.
Some HERV elements regulate gene expression in embryonic stem cells.
Some HERV proteins are expressed in the brain and may play roles in immune signaling.
There is active research on whether HERV reactivation contributes to autoimmune disease, neurodegeneration, and certain cancers — the picture is messy and not yet settled.
What I want you to take from this segment is a shift in framing.
Retroviruses are not just pathogens.
They are an evolutionary force.
They have been integrating into our genomes for hundreds of millions of years.
Most of those integrations are inert junk.
A small fraction have been domesticated and put to host use.
The boundary between "host" and "virus," at the genomic level, is much more porous than you might think.
